title: Motor Oil on Carpet
timeEstimate: 30 minutes to several hours plus fan-dry; used-oil carbon shadow may plateau
stainChemistry: Motor oil is a petroleum base-oil (C15–C50 hydrocarbons) + additive package — detergents, dispersants, anti-wear (ZDDP), viscosity modifiers. USED motor oil adds soot, combustion carbon, and wear-metal particulate from the engine — that carbon load is why used oil leaves a grey-black shadow long after the oil phase is extracted. Clean new oil is a standard hydrocarbon stain: dry-side solvent (POG / mineral spirits) dissolves it, surfactant emulsifies the residue. Used oil is a two-stage problem — the oil comes out; the carbon particulate can plateau. Carpet adds the pad-saturation failure mode: oil wicks down into the pad, then wicks BACK UP after you think you've extracted it. Weighted dry towel and patience between rounds matter more than chemistry choice.
whyThisWorks: Powder preload wicks the bulk oil up via capillary action before any chemistry. POG or mineral spirits dissolves the remaining hydrocarbon film. Dawn emulsifies residual oil and flushes the solvent. The limitation: used oil carbon particulate isn't a chemistry problem, it's a particulate problem — sometimes it only clears with pro hot-water extraction, and sometimes it's a permanent shadow.
defaultAssumption: Garage / driveway / workshop context. Fresh clean oil = reasonable DIY with powder + Dawn + patience. Used oil (dark, has been in an engine) = escalate earlier — carbon shadow is the failure mode home chemistry can't touch. Large spill or pad saturation = pro.

spottingProtocol.technique: Wick oil up via capillary — no chemistry yet
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cornstarch, baking soda, or fuller's earth; soft brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the oil stain with a thick layer of cornstarch, baking soda, or fuller's earth. Let sit 30–60 min. The powder wicks oil UP out of the carpet fibers. Vacuum off. Repeat with fresh powder if the first layer saturated.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent tamp from top — carpet has no back-access
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or mineral spirits on clean white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply solvent to a white cloth (never pour on carpet). Tamp from the top, flip cloth frequently, work outside-in toward the center. Ventilate the room. No flames. 2–3 passes for a fresh stain.

spottingProtocol.technique: Emulsify residual oil and flush solvent
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn dish soap + cool water, soft brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Work Dawn into the spot with a soft brush. Dwell 3–5 min. Dawn emulsifies residual oil and clears solvent residue. Rinse via damp cloth, not by pouring — over-wetting sinks the problem into the pad.

spottingProtocol.technique: Pull moisture + any residual oil out of the pad
spottingProtocol.equipment: Heavy dry white towel + weight (book / brick)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Stack dry white towels on the spot, weight them down, 30 min. Replace towels, repeat. This prevents oil from wicking back up from the pad.

spottingProtocol.technique: Dry the pad + check for carbon shadow
spottingProtocol.instruction: Fan the area fully dry. Inspect next day. If oil is gone but a grey-black shadow remains (used oil carbon), home chemistry is at its ceiling — pro hot-water extraction next.

steps: 1. Fuller's earth / cornstarch preload 30–60 min. Vacuum.
2. POG or mineral spirits tamp from top. Ventilate. No flames.
3. Dawn / NSD emulsify. Damp-cloth rinse — no pouring.
4. Weighted dry towel 30 min. Replace towels.
5. Fan-dry fully. Shadow remaining = hot-water extraction.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool / room temp throughout; no heat until oil is gone
warnings: Flammable solvent — ventilate, no flames, no smoking, no hot equipment
Never pour solvent on carpet — tamp with cloth only
Used motor oil carbon particulate may leave a permanent shadow
Never apply heat before oil phase is fully extracted — polymerizes

homeSolutions.agent: Is This Clean Oil or Used Oil?
homeSolutions.instruction: Clean / new motor oil (amber, clear) is a regular oil stain. USED motor oil (black, from an engine) carries soot and carbon from combustion — the oil may come out but leave a grey-black shadow that home chemistry can't fully clear. Set expectations: clean oil = reasonable home job. Used oil = pro-lane threshold is low.

Cover the stain with a thick layer of cornstarch, baking soda, or cat litter. Let it sit at least 30 minutes — an hour is better. The powder wicks the oil UP out of the carpet without any chemistry or water. Vacuum off. Much of the stain usually comes with it.

homeSolutions.agent: Solvent on a Rag — NOT Poured
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply Goo Gone or mineral spirits (from any hardware store) to a clean white rag. Press and tamp from the TOP of the carpet — never pour solvent on carpet. Work outside-in so you don't spread the edge. Open windows. No candles, no gas appliances running, no one smoking. Flip the rag frequently as it picks up oil.

Work a drop of Dawn into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Dawn emulsifies the remaining oil and clears the solvent. Rinse with a DAMP cloth, not by pouring water — pouring sends oil down into the pad where you can't reach it.

Stack paper towels on the spot, set a heavy book on top, leave 30 min. Swap for fresh ones, repeat. This pulls moisture AND any oil trying to wick back up from the pad. Overnight with fresh towels is the gold standard.

Point a fan at the spot and let it dry fully — usually overnight. Check it in daylight next day. Clean oil gone = win. Grey-black shadow remaining (used oil carbon) = home ceiling reached. Call a pro with hot-water extraction and manage expectations — used-oil shadows sometimes don't fully come out.

steps: 1. Clean oil = home job. Used oil = escalate early (carbon shadow).
3. Goo Gone / mineral spirits on a RAG, tamp from top. Ventilate. No flames.
4. Dawn + damp cloth. Never pour water on carpet.
5. Weighted paper towels 30 min, replace, repeat. Overnight best.
6. Fan-dry. Shadow remaining = pro hot-water extraction.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if used-oil carbon shadow persists after powder + solvent + Dawn, if pad is saturated, or if the spill covers more than a dinner plate.
warnings: FLAMMABLE SOLVENT — ventilate, no flames, no smoking, no gas stoves running
Never pour solvent or water on carpet — tamp and damp-cloth rinse
Used motor oil carbon may leave a permanent shadow
Never apply heat before oil phase is fully extracted

neverDo: Never use solvent near open flame, pilot light, or hot equipment
Never pour solvent on carpet — tamp from a cloth
Never over-wet carpet — pad saturation wicks oil back up
Never apply heat before the oil phase is extracted — polymerizes
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester carpet: tolerates POG, mineral spirits, Dawn
Olefin (polypropylene) carpet: some solvents soften olefin — inside-seam-test
Wool carpet: call a pro — pH-sensitive + solvent-risk

materialWarnings: Used oil carbon shadow often plateaus at home

escalation.when: Used-oil carbon shadow after full home cycle, pad saturation, spill larger than a dinner plate, wool carpet, or olefin carpet with visible solvent interaction.
escalation.whatToTell: "Motor oil on carpet — [clean / used], age [fresh / aged], home-treated with powder + Goo Gone + Dawn + weighted-towel extraction. [Oil cleared / carbon shadow remains / pad saturation suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction

contextQuestions: Clean new oil (amber) or used oil from an engine (black)?
How large is the stain — coin / dinner plate / larger?
Fresh or has it been sitting?
Any chance it soaked into the pad?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Used motor oil is the hard variant because carbon particulate is a physical problem, not a chemistry problem. Pad saturation is carpet's signature failure mode — weighted dry towels between rounds prevent wick-back.
